_meta.authoringNotes: Pediatric NS epidemiology from KDIGO 2021 Glomerular Diseases Guideline (Rovin Kidney Int 2021 PMID 34556300) and Trompeter NEJM 1985. Adult MCD per Hogan Clin J Am Soc Nephrol 2013 (PMID 23184567). Anti-nephrin antibody discovery (Watts NEJM 2022, PMID 36018559). Rituximab evidence: Iijima Lancet 2014 (PMID 24856612), Ravani Pediatrics 2011. KDIGO 2021 first-line steroid regimens for adults and children. PJP prophylaxis per Stoumpos Nephrol Dial Transplant 2018.

h1Title: Minimal Change Disease: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: Minimal change disease (MCD) is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (75-90% of pediatric cases) and the third most common in adults (10-15% of adult nephrotic syndrome). It is a podocytopathy with normal light microscopy and only diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes on electron microscopy, producing massive proteinuria with preserved kidney architecture. Children typically present at 2-6 years with sudden generalized edema and respond rapidly to oral prednisolone, with over 90% achieving remission within 4-8 weeks. Adults usually present later, take longer to respond, and have higher rates of acute kidney injury at diagnosis. The discovery of anti-nephrin autoantibodies in a significant subset of MCD patients (Watts NEJM 2022) has reframed it as an autoimmune podocytopathy. About 60-80% of children relapse at least once, and roughly one-third become frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent. Long-term outcomes are excellent when remissions are achieved, but cumulative steroid exposure drives most of the disease burden; rituximab and other steroid-sparing agents have become central to management of relapsing disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual pediatric nephrotic syndrome incidence approximately 2-7 per 100,000 children, of whom 75-90% have MCD; adult MCD incidence approximately 0.6 per 100,000
keyStats.demographics: Male:female ratio approximately 2:1 in children; closer to 1:1 in adults; appears in all ethnic groups with slightly higher rates in South Asian children
keyStats.avgAge: Pediatric peak 2-6 years (median age 3); adult MCD bimodal with peaks in 20s-30s and over 60
keyStats.globalCases: Several hundred thousand children worldwide at any time; substantial under-diagnosis in low- and middle-income countries

definition: Minimal change disease (ICD-10: N04.0 nephrotic syndrome with minor glomerular abnormality) is a glomerular podocytopathy that produces nephrotic-range proteinuria with normal or near-normal glomeruli on light microscopy and diffuse foot-process effacement of podocytes on electron microscopy. Immunofluorescence is typically negative or shows weak nonspecific deposits. The historical view that MCD was driven by an unidentified circulating permeability factor or T-cell cytokine is being refined by the discovery of anti-nephrin autoantibodies in a substantial subset of patients (Watts et al. NEJM 2022 and JASN 2024). Nephrin is the slit-diaphragm protein essential for podocyte filtration barrier integrity; autoantibodies disrupt slit-diaphragm function and trigger massive proteinuria. Primary MCD is idiopathic (or anti-nephrin-positive), while secondary MCD is associated with NSAIDs, lithium, interferon, bisphosphonates, Hodgkin lymphoma, viral infections, allergic disease, and IgA vasculitis. Diagnosis in children with classic presentation usually proceeds on empirical steroid therapy without biopsy; in adults and atypical cases, biopsy is mandatory. KDIGO 2021 categorizes pediatric MCD as steroid-sensitive (initial remission within 4 weeks of prednisolone), steroid-dependent (relapse during taper or within 2 weeks of stopping), frequently relapsing (2+ relapses within 6 months or 4+ within 12 months), and steroid-resistant (no remission after 8 weeks of full-dose steroids), each prompting different management.

typesClassification.description: 75-90% of childhood nephrotic syndrome. Typically responds to prednisolone 60 mg/m2/day within 2-4 weeks with complete remission. Long-term outlook excellent with steroid taper and treatment of relapses.
2 or more relapses within 6 months of initial response or 4+ within any 12-month period. Requires steroid-sparing therapy to minimize cumulative steroid toxicity.
Relapse occurs during steroid taper or within 2 weeks of stopping. Often progresses to FRNS pattern and benefits from steroid-sparing agents.
Failure to achieve remission within 8 weeks of full-dose prednisolone. Suspicion of FSGS, genetic podocytopathy (NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1), or atypical MCD; renal biopsy and genetic testing essential.

10-15% of adult nephrotic syndrome. Higher rates of acute kidney injury at diagnosis (30-40% in older adults), more delayed remission, and higher relapse rates than children. Same first-line treatment with steroids; rituximab increasingly used in relapsing disease.

Associated with NSAIDs, lithium, interferon, bisphosphonates, Hodgkin lymphoma (paraneoplastic), allergic disease, viral infections, and IgA vasculitis. Withdrawal of trigger or treatment of underlying disease often induces remission.

primarySymptoms: Sudden generalized edema developing over days to weeks, classically starting with periorbital swelling that parents mistake for an allergy.
Foamy or frothy urine that persists with successive flushes, reflecting heavy proteinuria.
Pitting edema of ankles, legs, scrotum or labia, and abdominal wall, with rapid weight gain.
Ascites and pleural effusions in severe cases, sometimes producing dyspnea or abdominal discomfort.
Reduced appetite, fatigue, and irritability, particularly in children with established nephrotic syndrome.
Mild hypertension (more common in adults than children).
Acute kidney injury at presentation in 30-40% of adults over 60 — often from intravascular volume depletion and tubular injury.
Recurrent infections, particularly streptococcal cellulitis, peritonitis (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis through ascitic fluid), and respiratory infections from urinary loss of immunoglobulin G.
Symptoms of complications: leg swelling and pain (DVT), pleuritic chest pain (PE), or flank pain and hematuria (renal vein thrombosis).
Symptoms of secondary causes when present — NSAID use, recent viral illness, allergic flare, malignancy-related systemic features.
earlyWarningSigns: Periorbital swelling that worsens over days and does not respond to antihistamines
Foamy urine seen by the child or parent over consecutive days
Sudden weight gain of more than 2-3 kg in a child or adult without changes in eating
Decreased urine output with full-feeling abdomen
Mild fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance in a previously healthy child
emergencySigns: Sudden severe abdominal pain with fever and ascites — exclude spontaneous bacterial peritonitis with diagnostic paracentesis and treat with broad-spectrum antibiotics
Acute oliguria with rising creatinine in an edematous child or adult — assess volume status and exclude AKI from hypovolemia or tubular injury
Sudden severe headache, focal neurological deficit, or seizures — exclude cerebral venous thrombosis in a hypercoagulable nephrotic patient
Pleuritic chest pain or dyspnea — exclude pulmonary embolism with CT pulmonary angiography
High fever with respiratory distress in a child on high-dose steroids — exclude varicella, pneumonia, or other opportunistic infection

causes.cause: Anti-nephrin autoantibodies (in a significant subset of primary MCD)
causes.description: Autoantibodies against nephrin, the slit-diaphragm protein at the podocyte, disrupt the filtration barrier and trigger massive proteinuria. The Watts NEJM 2022 study identified anti-nephrin antibodies in many primary MCD patients, refining MCD as an autoimmune podocytopathy and explaining steroid sensitivity.

Decades of work supported the hypothesis that an unidentified circulating cytokine or T-cell product alters glomerular permeability. The model is being integrated with anti-nephrin findings but remains relevant for non-anti-nephrin MCD.

Chronic NSAID use can cause MCD with or without acute interstitial nephritis; lithium, interferon, bisphosphonates (especially pamidronate), and some antibiotics are recognized triggers. Drug withdrawal often induces remission.

Paraneoplastic MCD is well described, particularly with Hodgkin disease; remission of MCD often follows successful cancer treatment. Older adults with new MCD should be screened with appropriate imaging.
MCD often follows or coexists with allergic flares (eczema, asthma, food allergy, insect stings). Allergic mechanism is unproven but the clinical association is consistent.
Upper respiratory tract infections, COVID-19, and parvovirus B19 have all preceded MCD episodes; vaccination has rarely been associated with onset or relapse in case reports.

riskFactors.description: Peak incidence in early childhood; presentations under 1 year prompt evaluation for congenital nephrotic syndrome and inherited podocytopathies.
Male:female ratio approximately 2:1 in childhood disease; sex distribution evens out in adults.
Personal or family history of asthma, eczema, food allergy, and allergic rhinitis is more common in children with MCD; allergic flares can precipitate relapses.
Long-term NSAID use is a recognized adult cause; discontinuation often induces remission. Important to screen at diagnosis.
Up to 5-10% of adult MCD over age 60 is paraneoplastic. Lymphoma should be excluded by examination and imaging.
Recent URTI, COVID-19, and rarely vaccinations precede first presentation or relapse. Maintaining usual immunizations remains recommended; live vaccines are deferred during heavy immunosuppression.
Both cause secondary MCD; review of pharmacy records is important in adults.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 2-6 years account for the majority of cases; presentations under 1 year are rare and require evaluation for inherited disease.
Adults: median age 30-50 with a second peak over 60; older adults more often have AKI at presentation and slower response to therapy.
South Asian children have slightly higher reported rates; African and Hispanic children more often have FSGS as the underlying biopsy diagnosis when steroid-resistant.
Approximately 60-80% of pediatric patients relapse at least once after initial remission.
Long-term renal survival is excellent (over 95% maintain normal renal function) in steroid-responsive patients.
diagnosisOverview: In a child aged 1-12 with sudden generalized edema, heavy proteinuria, and hypoalbuminemia, the diagnosis is presumed MCD and empirical prednisolone is started without biopsy. KDIGO 2021 recommends biopsy only when atypical features are present: age under 1 year, persistent hypertension, persistent microscopic hematuria, AKI without obvious reversible cause, low complement, signs of systemic disease, or failure to respond to 4-8 weeks of full-dose steroids. In adults, renal biopsy is mandatory because of the broader differential (FSGS, membranous nephropathy, IgA nephropathy, diabetic nephropathy, paraprotein-related disease). Biopsy shows normal or near-normal glomeruli on light microscopy, diffuse foot-process effacement on electron microscopy, and either no immune deposits or only nonspecific findings on immunofluorescence. Laboratory workup includes serum albumin (typically under 25 g/L), serum creatinine and eGFR, lipid profile (hyperlipidemia universal), full blood count, complement (normal in primary MCD; low complement suggests lupus or post-infectious glomerulonephritis), autoimmune screen, hepatitis B and C serology, HIV testing, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio or 24-hour collection, urine microscopy, and renal ultrasound. In adults over 50, age-appropriate cancer screening and serum/urine immunofixation are added. Anti-nephrin antibody testing is emerging at specialty centers. Genetic testing for podocin (NPHS2), nephrin (NPHS1), WT1, and TRPC6 mutations is offered in suspected steroid-resistant or familial disease and in children under 1 year. Stratification into steroid-sensitive, steroid-dependent, frequently relapsing, or steroid-resistant disease is based on response to the initial treatment and recurrence pattern.

diagnosticTests.test: 24-hour urine protein or urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms nephrotic-range proteinuria (over 3.5 g/24h adult, urinary protein-to-creatinine ratio over 2 g/g in children)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Children typically use spot urine PCR; adults a 24-hour collection. Results 1-2 days. Serial measurements track remission and relapse.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum albumin, creatinine, eGFR, lipid profile, FBC
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms nephrotic syndrome and assesses baseline function and thrombosis risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results same day. Albumin under 25 g/L raises VTE risk.

diagnosticTests.test: Renal biopsy (mandatory in adults, atypical pediatrics)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes MCD from FSGS, membranous nephropathy, lupus, and other causes; assesses chronic damage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case procedure under local anesthesia and ultrasound guidance, 30 minutes plus 6-12 hours observation. Histology report within 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Complement (C3, C4), autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-dsDNA), HBV/HCV/HIV serology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes lupus, post-infectious GN, hepatitis-related secondary nephrotic syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw with results in 1-2 weeks. Particularly important in adolescents and adults with new nephrotic syndrome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Emerging biomarker that identifies an autoimmune subset of MCD; may guide rituximab use and predict relapse
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialty lab testing; turnaround 1-3 weeks. Not yet a routine clinical test in most centers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies inherited podocytopathies in steroid-resistant or congenital disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood or saliva sample to specialty molecular lab. Results 4-8 weeks; pre- and post-test counseling recommended.

diagnosticTests.test: Age-appropriate cancer screening (adults over 50, especially older adults)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes Hodgkin lymphoma, solid tumors that can cause paraneoplastic MCD
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Coordinated outpatient workup including CT thorax/abdomen/pelvis, colonoscopy, mammography, PSA as appropriate.

treatmentOverview: First-line therapy is oral corticosteroids. KDIGO 2021 recommends prednisolone 60 mg/m2/day (max 60 mg) in children for 4-6 weeks, followed by 40 mg/m2 every other day for 2-5 months, with a total course of about 12-16 weeks. Adults receive prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (max 80 mg) for up to 16 weeks until remission, then tapered over 6 months. Over 90% of children and 70-80% of adults achieve complete remission, defined as proteinuria under 0.3 g/day. Relapse is common: 60-80% of children have at least one relapse, and 30-40% become frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent. Steroid-sparing agents reduce cumulative steroid exposure and toxicity. Cyclophosphamide 2-3 mg/kg/day for 8-12 weeks induces sustained remission in 60-70% of frequently relapsing children but is rarely repeated due to gonadal and oncogenic risk. Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine 5-6 mg/kg/day or tacrolimus 0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day, trough levels 60-100 ng/mL for cyclosporine) plus low-dose prednisolone maintain remission in 60-70% but require monitoring for nephrotoxicity. Mycophenolate mofetil is an alternative with fewer toxicity concerns. Rituximab (375 mg/m2 weekly × 2-4 doses or 1 g IV days 1 and 15) has emerged as a preferred steroid-sparing agent for frequently relapsing and steroid-dependent disease — Iijima Lancet 2014 showed superiority over placebo for sustained remission in pediatric SDNS, and adult RCTs have followed. Levamisole is used in some centers as a low-cost option in children. Supportive care includes ACE inhibitor or ARB for any residual proteinuria, sodium restriction (under 2-3 g/day), diuretics, statin for hyperlipidemia, antibiotic and PJP prophylaxis during heavy immunosuppression, and consideration of anticoagulation in high-risk adults. Vaccination updates including pneumococcal, varicella, and influenza are essential between immunosuppression cycles. Long-term care monitors growth (in children), bone density (in adults on steroids), cataracts, infections, and renal function.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone (60 mg/m2/day in children, 1 mg/kg/day in adults, up to 60-80 mg) for initial therapy
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy. Daily dose for 4-6 weeks until remission, then alternate-day taper over 12-20 weeks (KDIGO 2021). Most children remit within 4 weeks; adults often take 8-16 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete remission in over 90% of children and 70-80% of adults within 4-16 weeks (Trompeter 1985; Hogan CJASN 2013)

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclophosphamide (2-3 mg/kg/day oral or 500-750 mg/m2 IV monthly × 6) for FRNS/SDNS
medicalTreatments.description: Induces sustained remission in many children with frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent disease. Limited to one course because of cumulative gonadal and oncogenic toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained remission at 2 years in 50-70% of frequently relapsing children (KDIGO 2021)

medicalTreatments.name: Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine 5-6 mg/kg/day or tacrolimus 0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day) plus low-dose steroid
medicalTreatments.description: Standard steroid-sparing therapy for FRNS, SDNS, and selected steroid-resistant cases. Requires monitoring of trough levels, blood pressure, and renal function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained remission in 60-70% at 1-2 years (Cattran 2007); relapse common after withdrawal

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (375 mg/m2 weekly × 2-4 doses or 1 g IV days 1 and 15)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody depleting B cells. Increasingly first-line for FRNS, SDNS, and as steroid-sparing in adults. Repeated dosing guided by B-cell recovery and clinical relapse.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained remission at 1 year in 60-80% of pediatric SDNS in randomized trials (Iijima Lancet 2014, Ravani Pediatrics 2011)

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil (600-1200 mg/m2/day in children, 1-2 g/day in adults)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing agent useful when calcineurin inhibitors are contraindicated. Lower nephrotoxicity but generally less effective.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained remission in 40-60% of FRNS at 1 year (Banerjee Pediatr Nephrol 2017)

medicalTreatments.name: Levamisole 2-2.5 mg/kg every other day (pediatric)
medicalTreatments.description: Low-cost immunomodulator widely used in many countries for FRNS in children. KDIGO 2021 recognizes Level 1B evidence for benefit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained remission in approximately 50% of FRNS at 1-2 years (Gellermann JASN 2013)

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitor or ARB at maximum tolerated dose
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces residual proteinuria and slows CKD progression. Used in patients with persistent or partial proteinuria.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Proteinuria reduction 20-30% additional on top of remission therapy

alternativeTreatments.name: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies beyond rituximab (ofatumumab, obinutuzumab)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in rituximab-resistant cases or trial settings; show deeper B-cell depletion. Evidence is growing but still off-label for many indications.

alternativeTreatments.name: Plasma exchange in steroid-resistant disease with suspected circulating factor
alternativeTreatments.description: Reserved for rapidly progressive steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome where biopsy suggests MCD or FSGS; theoretical removal of circulating permeability factor. Evidence base limited.

whySeeSpecialist: Pediatric nephrology referral is required for any child with nephrotic syndrome. Adult nephrology evaluation is necessary because adults require biopsy and broader differential workup. Specialist input is essential for steroid-sparing regimens, transplant assessment in rare progressive cases, and management of complications.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a pediatric or adult nephrologist with experience in glomerular diseases and KDIGO-aligned protocols. Tertiary centers with access to renal pathology, electron microscopy, rituximab infusion capacity, anti-nephrin antibody testing, and clinical trials are preferred for relapsing or steroid-resistant disease.
hospitalCriteria: Pediatric or adult nephrology service with renal biopsy capability
Access to rituximab and clinical-trial enrollment for relapsing disease
Pediatric care pathway with vaccination and growth monitoring
keyFacilities: Renal transplant centers (for rare progressive cases)
Lymphoma and oncology services for paraneoplastic MCD
Rheumatology and lupus clinics for atypical adult cases
preventionStrategies: Maintain routine immunizations (especially pneumococcal, varicella, influenza, COVID-19) between immunosuppression cycles to reduce infection risk.
Avoid NSAID use in patients with MCD; alternative analgesia under medical guidance.
Monitor children with first nephrotic episode for early signs of relapse (foamy urine, weight gain, edema) and start home urine dipstick monitoring.
Engage with parent/patient education programs for early recognition and treatment of relapses.
Maintain vaccination updates for household contacts of immunosuppressed patients.
lifestyleModifications: Restrict dietary salt to 2-3 g/day during edematous phases to control fluid retention.
Adhere strictly to the steroid taper schedule; never stop abruptly without medical guidance.
Take infection prophylaxis (PJP cover, sometimes pneumococcal) during heavy immunosuppression.
Test home urine with dipstick during relapse-prone periods to detect relapse early.
Attend all monitoring appointments for steroid side effects (growth, bone density, cataract).
Stay physically active within tolerance; resume normal exercise during remission.

recommended: Sodium 2-3 g/day (less than 5 g salt) during edematous phases
Adequate protein 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day to support growth and replace urinary loss
Calcium 1000-1200 mg/day and vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day during steroid therapy
Mediterranean-style pattern with vegetables, fruits, whole grains, fish
avoid: Excess sodium (over 5 g salt/day) and processed food during active disease
Grapefruit juice in patients on cyclosporine or tacrolimus
NSAIDs and high-dose ibuprofen for pain — alternative analgesia recommended
Excess sugar and refined carbohydrate during steroid therapy to limit weight gain

exerciseGuidelines: Children and adults can resume normal exercise during remission. Avoid contact sports if on anticoagulation. Resistance training during steroid courses helps maintain lean mass. During heavy immunosuppression, avoid prolonged crowded indoor sports settings during respiratory virus season.
dailyManagement: Take steroids with food each morning; monitor for mood, sleep, and weight changes.
Test urine with dipstick if foamy urine, weight gain, or edema recurs.
Maintain calcium, vitamin D, and bone protection during long courses of steroids.
Attend regular nephrology appointments; bring growth charts (children) or weight diary (adults).
Monitor blood pressure at home twice weekly during active therapy.
Maintain hand hygiene and avoid close contact with known infections during immunosuppression.
prognosis: Outlook is excellent. Over 95% of pediatric steroid-sensitive MCD patients maintain normal renal function long-term, and most outgrow relapses by adulthood. About 60-80% relapse at least once, 30-40% become frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent in childhood, and 5-10% are steroid-resistant — the latter often have underlying FSGS or genetic disease and worse outcomes. Adult-onset MCD has slower remission times, higher rates of AKI at presentation (30-40% over age 60), and higher relapse rates than children but maintained renal function in over 90% with appropriate therapy. Major long-term concerns are cumulative steroid toxicity (growth retardation in children, bone disease, cataracts, hypertension, weight gain, mood changes, diabetes) and infection during heavy immunosuppression. Steroid-sparing agents (cyclophosphamide, calcineurin inhibitors, rituximab, MMF, levamisole) reduce cumulative steroid exposure substantially. ESRD is rare in true MCD (under 5% over 20 years).
recoveryTimeline: Initial remission in children typically within 2-4 weeks of starting prednisolone; adults take 8-16 weeks. Edema resolves within 1-2 weeks of treatment response. Steroid taper occurs over 12-20 weeks for first episode. Sustained remission can last years; relapses are managed with re-treatment. Rituximab effect begins within 1-2 weeks of infusion with peak B-cell depletion lasting 6-12 months; repeat dosing guided by B-cell recovery and relapse pattern.
complications: Infection from urinary loss of immunoglobulins and complement: cellulitis, pneumonia, peritonitis, encapsulated organism sepsis (pneumococcus, Haemophilus, meningococcus).
Thromboembolism (renal vein thrombosis, DVT, PE) in 5-10% of severe nephrotic syndrome, mostly adults with albumin under 25 g/L.
Cumulative steroid toxicity: growth retardation, weight gain, hypertension, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, cataracts, mood disturbance.
Acute kidney injury at presentation, particularly in adults over 60, from intravascular volume depletion and tubular injury.
Hypovolemic shock from over-diuresis or sepsis superimposed on heavy proteinuria.
Progression to FSGS pattern with chronic kidney disease in a small subset of steroid-resistant patients.

supportResources.name: NephCure Kidney International
supportResources.description: Patient organization for nephrotic syndrome with educational resources, support groups, and research updates.

supportResources.name: International Pediatric Nephrology Association (IPNA)
supportResources.description: Global pediatric nephrology society with care pathways for steroid-sensitive nephrotic syndrome.

supportResources.name: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guidelines 2021
supportResources.description: Evidence-based international guidance on management of MCD and other glomerular diseases.

supportResources.name: National Kidney Foundation — Nephrotic Syndrome
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of nephrotic syndrome, including minimal change disease, for adults and families.

confusedWithConditions.name: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: FSGS shows segmental scarring of glomeruli with podocyte injury on light microscopy, often with partial steroid response and higher rates of progression to ESRD. MCD has normal light microscopy and excellent steroid response. Both can show foot-process effacement on electron microscopy and require biopsy to distinguish, especially in steroid-resistant disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Membranous nephropathy is the commonest adult nephrotic-syndrome cause and shows subepithelial immune deposits with granular IgG along the GBM. MCD has no immune deposits and shows only podocyte foot-process effacement. Anti-PLA2R antibody helps confirm membranous.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Class V lupus nephritis can mimic primary nephrotic syndrome; positive ANA, anti-dsDNA, low complement, and systemic features (rash, joint pain) distinguish lupus. MCD has normal complement and no systemic autoimmune features.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IgA nephropathy classically presents with hematuria and episodic flares triggered by infection, with mesangial IgA deposits on biopsy. MCD presents with nephrotic-range proteinuria, normal light microscopy, and negative immunofluorescence.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrotic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Clinical syndrome that MCD typically produces; differential includes FSGS, membranous nephropathy, lupus, and diabetic nephropathy.

relatedConditions.name: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Closest histologic and clinical relative; both are podocytopathies and may exist on a spectrum.

relatedConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common adult nephrotic-syndrome cause; distinguished by biopsy and anti-PLA2R testing.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Rare long-term consequence of steroid-resistant or repeatedly relapsing MCD.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis B
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary cause that must be excluded with serology at diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is minimal change disease?
faqs.answer: Minimal change disease is a kidney disease causing nephrotic syndrome with massive protein loss in the urine. Under the microscope, the kidney looks normal except for changes in podocyte foot processes visible only on electron microscopy. It is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children and responds well to corticosteroids.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of minimal change disease?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include sudden generalized swelling (often starting around the eyes), foamy urine, rapid weight gain, fatigue, and reduced appetite. Adults may develop acute kidney injury or recurrent infections. Some patients have abdominal pain from ascites or breathlessness from pleural effusions.

faqs.question: How is minimal change disease diagnosed?
faqs.answer: In a child with classic nephrotic syndrome, empirical steroid therapy is started without biopsy. Adults and atypical cases require renal biopsy showing normal glomeruli on light microscopy and foot-process effacement on electron microscopy. Blood tests assess kidney function, albumin, lipids, complement, and screen for secondary causes.

faqs.question: How effective are steroids for MCD?
faqs.answer: Steroids induce complete remission in over 90% of children and 70-80% of adults with minimal change disease. The typical course is prednisolone for 12-20 weeks with gradual taper. Children usually remit within 4 weeks; adults often take 8-16 weeks.

faqs.question: Do MCD relapses happen often?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 60-80% of children with steroid-sensitive MCD relapse at least once, and 30-40% become frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent. Each relapse is treated with re-introduction of prednisolone; persistent relapses prompt steroid-sparing therapy such as cyclophosphamide, rituximab, or a calcineurin inhibitor.

faqs.question: What is steroid-dependent nephrotic syndrome?
faqs.answer: Steroid-dependent nephrotic syndrome relapses during steroid taper or within 2 weeks of stopping. It is a common pattern in MCD and significantly increases cumulative steroid exposure. Steroid-sparing agents such as rituximab, cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate, or calcineurin inhibitors are added to maintain remission.

faqs.question: Is rituximab effective in MCD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Rituximab is now a preferred steroid-sparing agent for frequently relapsing and steroid-dependent MCD. The Iijima Lancet 2014 trial in children showed superior sustained remission at 1 year versus placebo, and adult evidence is accumulating. Doses are 375 mg/m2 weekly × 2-4 or 1 g IV on days 1 and 15.

faqs.question: What is anti-nephrin antibody in MCD?
faqs.answer: Anti-nephrin antibodies are autoantibodies against the podocyte slit-diaphragm protein nephrin. The Watts NEJM 2022 study identified them in a substantial subset of MCD patients, reframing MCD as an autoimmune podocytopathy. Testing is currently available at specialty labs and may guide steroid-sparing therapy in the future.

faqs.question: Can MCD lead to kidney failure?
faqs.answer: True steroid-sensitive MCD rarely progresses to end-stage kidney disease (under 5% over 20 years). Steroid-resistant cases often have underlying FSGS or genetic disease and worse outcomes. Most children outgrow relapses by adulthood and maintain normal renal function long-term.

faqs.question: What causes minimal change disease?
faqs.answer: Most primary MCD is autoimmune, with anti-nephrin autoantibodies identified in many patients. Secondary causes include NSAIDs, lithium, interferon, bisphosphonates, Hodgkin lymphoma, viral infections (URTI, COVID-19), and allergic disease. A small proportion is associated with inherited podocyte gene mutations.

faqs.question: Do adults with MCD have different outcomes from children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults often take longer to respond (8-16 weeks versus 2-4 in children), have higher rates of AKI at presentation (30-40% over age 60), and higher relapse rates. Renal survival is still over 90% with appropriate therapy. Rituximab is increasingly used in relapsing adult disease.

faqs.question: Is biopsy always needed for MCD?
faqs.answer: Not in children with classic presentation — empirical steroid therapy is the standard. Biopsy is required in adults, in atypical pediatric cases (under 1 year, persistent hypertension, hematuria, AKI, low complement), and when patients do not respond to 4-8 weeks of full-dose steroids.

faqs.question: Are NSAIDs safe with MCD?
faqs.answer: No. NSAIDs and naproxen can cause or worsen MCD and reduce kidney function. They should be avoided in active disease and used cautiously, if at all, during remission. Alternative analgesia (paracetamol, opioids in specialist guidance, gabapentinoids) is recommended.

faqs.question: Should children with MCD avoid vaccinations?
faqs.answer: Routine immunizations including pneumococcal, varicella, influenza, and COVID-19 are recommended; they reduce infection risk during nephrotic flares. Live vaccines are deferred during high-dose steroid therapy and within 6 months of rituximab. Household contacts should also be vaccinated.

faqs.question: How do I know if MCD is relapsing?
faqs.answer: Relapse is usually heralded by foamy urine, weight gain, and reappearance of edema. Home urine dipstick (over 2+ protein on 3 consecutive mornings) confirms relapse. Early recognition allows prompt re-treatment and reduces severity. Parents and patients are taught dipstick monitoring at diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can adults outgrow MCD?
faqs.answer: About one-third of adults achieve sustained remission off therapy after several years. Relapses become less frequent over time. Older adults are at higher risk of AKI and slower remission but renal function recovers in most. Long-term steroid-sparing therapy reduces cumulative toxicity.

faqs.question: What are the long-term risks of steroids for MCD?
faqs.answer: Cumulative steroid exposure causes growth retardation in children, bone disease (osteoporosis), cataracts, hypertension, hyperglycemia, weight gain, mood changes, and infection susceptibility. Steroid-sparing agents (cyclophosphamide, rituximab, calcineurin inhibitors, MMF, levamisole) are central to minimizing these toxicities in relapsing disease.

faqs.question: Can MCD be linked to cancer in adults?
faqs.answer: Hodgkin lymphoma and certain solid tumors can cause paraneoplastic MCD, particularly in adults over 60. Treatment of the malignancy often induces remission of MCD. Age-appropriate cancer screening (chest CT, colonoscopy, mammography, PSA) is recommended in older adults at diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is exercise safe during MCD treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children and adults can return to normal physical activity during remission. Resistance training helps maintain lean mass during steroid therapy. Patients on anticoagulation should avoid contact sports. Vigorous activity during severe edema or AKI should be deferred until clinical stability.

faqs.question: What diet helps with minimal change disease?
faqs.answer: Restrict salt to 2-3 g/day during edema. Adequate protein 1.0-1.2 g/kg/day supports growth and replaces urinary losses. Calcium and vitamin D protect bone during steroid therapy. Avoid NSAIDs and grapefruit juice (if on calcineurin inhibitors). A renal dietitian provides individualized advice.

faqs.question: Can MCD recur after a kidney transplant?
faqs.answer: Recurrence in the allograft is uncommon (under 10%) for true MCD, much lower than for primary FSGS. When MCD does recur, it often responds to steroids and rituximab. Pre-transplant biopsy and genetic testing help distinguish MCD from FSGS in cases destined for transplant.

simpleName: Minimal Change Disease (MCD, lipoid nephrosis, nil disease)
metaTitle: Minimal Change Disease: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Minimal change disease (MCD): commonest pediatric nephrotic syndrome. Steroid response, relapses, rituximab and steroid-sparing options, prognosis explained.
